meta.title: DBX-DEA-3: DLT, Workflows and Production Operations

domains.name: ELT with Spark SQL and Python

questions.stem: How does a data warehouse differ from a data lake in terms of schema enforcement?

options.text: A data warehouse enforces schema on write; a data lake typically applies schema on read
A data lake enforces schema on write; a data warehouse applies schema on read
Both enforce schema on write but use different file formats
Neither enforces schema; both store raw data without any type constraints

questions.explanation: A is correct. Traditional data warehouses enforce schema on write — data must conform to the defined table structure before it can be ingested. Data lakes typically apply schema on read, meaning raw files are stored without enforcement and the schema is interpreted at query time. This is why data lakes are more flexible for raw storage but can lead to data quality issues. The Lakehouse (with Delta Lake) bridges this by adding schema enforcement to object storage. B is incorrect because the descriptions are reversed. C is incorrect because they differ in schema timing, not just file format. D is incorrect because data warehouses definitively enforce schema on write.
questions.reference: Data warehouse vs data lake: schema on write vs schema on read

questions.stem: A data engineer terminates a job cluster after a scheduled job finishes. When will this cluster be available again?

options.text: The cluster stays in a hibernated state and is available immediately on the next job run
The cluster is permanently deleted and a new cluster must be created for the next job run
Job clusters are created automatically at the start of a job run and terminated automatically after the run completes — they are not manually managed
The cluster is moved to a warm pool and can be manually restarted within 24 hours

questions.explanation: C is correct. Job clusters are ephemeral by design — Databricks automatically creates a new cluster when a job starts and terminates it after the job completes. Engineers do not manually create or terminate job clusters; this lifecycle is handled by the job scheduler. A is incorrect because job clusters do not hibernate; they terminate. B is incorrect because the phrasing 'permanently deleted and must be created' implies manual management, whereas job clusters are created fresh automatically each run from the job's cluster specification. D is incorrect because Databricks does not have a warm pool feature that holds terminated job clusters for manual restart.
questions.reference: Databricks cluster types: job cluster lifecycle

questions.stem:
A junior data engineer uses the following in a notebook to switch from Python to SQL mid-notebook:

```
%sql
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE status = 'shipped';
```

What is true about this magic command?

options.text: The `%sql` magic applies only to the current cell, reverting to the notebook's default language for the next cell
The `%sql` magic changes the default language for the entire notebook for all subsequent cells
The `%sql` magic can only be used in the first cell of a notebook
The `%sql` magic is deprecated and should be replaced with `spark.sql()` in Python cells

questions.explanation: A is correct. Magic commands like `%sql`, `%python`, `%scala`, and `%r` are cell-level directives that override the notebook's default language for that single cell only. The next cell reverts to the notebook's configured default language. This allows mixing languages within the same notebook without changing the notebook's default. B is incorrect because magic commands are cell-scoped, not notebook-scoped — to change the notebook default, you use the language selector in the notebook settings. C is incorrect because magic commands can appear in any cell. D is incorrect because `%sql` is a supported and non-deprecated feature.
questions.reference: Databricks notebooks: language magic commands

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to enable autoscaling on an all-purpose cluster with a minimum of 2 workers and a maximum of 8 workers. The workload is variable — heavy during business hours and light overnight. What is the PRIMARY benefit of autoscaling in this scenario?

options.text: Autoscaling ensures 100% uptime by automatically replacing failed worker nodes
Autoscaling adds workers when the cluster is under heavy load and removes them during idle periods, optimizing cost and performance
Autoscaling automatically upgrades the Databricks Runtime version when new releases are available
Autoscaling distributes network traffic between multiple clusters running in parallel

questions.explanation: B is correct. Autoscaling dynamically adjusts the number of worker nodes between the configured minimum and maximum based on current cluster utilization. During peak business hours, it scales up to handle heavy workloads; during overnight idle periods, it scales down to reduce cost. A is incorrect because autoscaling is about scaling capacity, not node failure replacement (which is handled by node replacement/spot fallback). C is incorrect because autoscaling has nothing to do with runtime version management. D is incorrect because autoscaling adds/removes nodes from a single cluster; it does not distribute traffic across multiple clusters.
questions.reference: Databricks cluster autoscaling

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to enable schema evolution when writing a new DataFrame to an existing Delta table that has a different set of columns. Select TWO approaches that allow new columns in the DataFrame to be automatically added to the Delta table schema. (Select TWO)

options.text: Use `.option('mergeSchema', 'true')` in the DataFrame writer
Set the Spark configuration `spark.databricks.delta.schema.autoMerge.enabled` to `true`
Use `mode('overwrite')` to replace the existing schema entirely
Drop and recreate the Delta table before each write operation

questions.explanation: A and B are correct. Delta Lake provides two ways to enable schema evolution (automatic merging of new columns): (1) the `mergeSchema` write option set on the DataFrame writer for a per-write override, and (2) the Spark configuration `spark.databricks.delta.schema.autoMerge.enabled` set to `true` for session-level enablement. Both approaches allow the target Delta table schema to be updated to include new columns from the incoming DataFrame. C is incorrect because `mode('overwrite')` replaces the data (and optionally the schema), but it does not perform schema merging — it discards the old schema unless `overwriteSchema` is also set. D is incorrect because dropping and recreating the table is destructive and not schema evolution.
questions.reference: Delta Lake schema evolution: mergeSchema option

questions.stem: A data engineer uses Databricks Repos to manage notebook code. They want to pull the latest changes from the main branch in GitHub to their Databricks Repo. Which action in the Repos UI accomplishes this?

options.text: Click 'Commit & Push' to upload local changes to GitHub
Click 'Pull' to fetch and merge the latest changes from the remote branch into the Databricks Repo
Click 'Create Branch' to start a new branch from the current state
Click 'Export' to download the notebook files to a local machine

questions.explanation: B is correct. The 'Pull' operation in the Databricks Repos UI performs a `git pull` — it fetches the latest commits from the remote branch (e.g., GitHub's `main`) and merges them into the current local branch in the Databricks Repo. A is incorrect because 'Commit & Push' sends local changes to the remote, which is the opposite direction. C is incorrect because 'Create Branch' creates a new branch from the current commit, not pulls from remote. D is incorrect because 'Export' downloads notebook files but does not sync with Git.
questions.reference: Databricks Repos: pulling changes from remote

questions.stem:
A Delta table is queried with:

```sql
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE order_date = '2024-03-15'
  AND region = 'WEST';
```

The table is not partitioned and has no Z-ORDER applied. What mechanism does Delta Lake use to avoid reading all Parquet files for this query?

options.text: Delta Lake uses a B-tree index on `order_date` and `region` to find matching files
Delta Lake stores min/max column statistics per Parquet file in the transaction log and uses them to skip files that cannot contain matching rows (data skipping)
Delta Lake uses full table scans because only Z-ORDER can enable file-level pruning
Delta Lake caches the first query result and returns it directly for identical subsequent queries

questions.explanation: B is correct. Delta Lake collects column-level min/max statistics (and null counts) for each Parquet file and stores them in the transaction log. When a query has filter predicates, Delta's query optimizer checks these statistics to determine which files could possibly contain matching rows and skips all other files. This is called data skipping. Z-ORDER improves data skipping by co-locating related rows, but basic data skipping via statistics works even without Z-ORDER. A is incorrect because Delta Lake does not use B-tree indexes by default (Bloom filter indexes are available but not B-trees). C is incorrect because data skipping works without Z-ORDER. D is incorrect because Delta Lake does not cache query results as described.
questions.reference: Delta Lake: data skipping via column statistics

questions.stem:
A data engineer creates a table:

```sql
CREATE TABLE product_summary
USING DELTA
AS SELECT product_id, SUM(quantity) AS total_qty
FROM order_items
GROUP BY product_id;
```

A week later, new rows are added to `order_items`. What is true about `product_summary`?

options.text: `product_summary` automatically updates to reflect the new rows in `order_items`
`product_summary` contains the aggregated data from when the CTAS statement ran and does NOT automatically reflect new rows in `order_items`
`product_summary` is a view that re-executes the aggregation query each time it is queried
`product_summary` will fail future queries because the source table `order_items` has been modified

questions.explanation: B is correct. `CREATE TABLE AS SELECT` (CTAS) materializes the query result as a physical Delta table at the time of execution. It is a one-time snapshot and has no ongoing relationship with `order_items`. New rows added to `order_items` are NOT reflected in `product_summary` unless the table is explicitly refreshed (e.g., by running the CTAS again with `CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE`). A is incorrect because physical tables do not auto-update from source tables. C is incorrect because a CTAS creates a table, not a view. D is incorrect because `product_summary` is independent — changes to `order_items` do not affect queries on `product_summary`.
questions.reference: CTAS: static snapshot behavior

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to add a column `tax_amount` (computed as `price * 0.08`) to an existing DataFrame `df` without modifying the source table. Which PySpark operation should they use?

options.text: `df.addColumn('tax_amount', df.price * 0.08)`
`df.withColumn('tax_amount', df.price * 0.08)`
`df.insertColumn('tax_amount', df.price * 0.08)`
`df.select('tax_amount = price * 0.08')`

questions.explanation: B is correct. `DataFrame.withColumn(name, expression)` returns a new DataFrame with an additional column (or replaces a column of the same name). It does not modify the source DataFrame or the underlying table. A is incorrect because `addColumn` is not a valid PySpark DataFrame method. C is incorrect because `insertColumn` is not a valid PySpark DataFrame method. D is incorrect because `df.select('tax_amount = price * 0.08')` treats the string as a column name, not an expression — the correct SQL expression approach would be `df.selectExpr('*', 'price * 0.08 AS tax_amount')`.
questions.reference: PySpark DataFrame: withColumn for computed columns

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to update existing rows and insert new rows into a Delta table based on a source DataFrame, using `customer_id` as the key. Which SQL command is MOST appropriate?

options.text: `INSERT INTO customers SELECT * FROM new_customers`
`MERGE INTO customers USING new_customers ON customers.customer_id = new_customers.customer_id WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET * WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *`
`CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE customers AS SELECT * FROM new_customers`
`UPDATE customers SET * FROM new_customers WHERE customers.customer_id = new_customers.customer_id`

questions.explanation: B is correct. `MERGE INTO` (also known as upsert) is the correct SQL command for updating existing rows when a match is found on the key and inserting new rows when no match exists. This is a fundamental operation for maintaining slowly changing dimension tables and incremental loads. A is incorrect because `INSERT INTO` only adds new rows — it does not update existing rows with matching keys. C is incorrect because `CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE ... AS SELECT` replaces the entire table with only the new source data, losing any rows that exist in `customers` but not in `new_customers`. D is incorrect because the `UPDATE ... FROM` syntax in the form shown is not valid standard Spark SQL.
questions.reference: MERGE INTO for upsert operations in Delta Lake

questions.stem: A data engineer queries a table where `phone_number` is sometimes `NULL`. They want rows with `NULL` phone numbers to appear LAST when sorting. Which `ORDER BY` clause achieves this?

options.text: `ORDER BY phone_number ASC`
`ORDER BY phone_number ASC NULLS LAST`
`ORDER BY COALESCE(phone_number, 'ZZZ') ASC`
`ORDER BY phone_number DESC NULLS FIRST`

questions.explanation: B is correct. Spark SQL supports `NULLS FIRST` and `NULLS LAST` modifiers on `ORDER BY` clauses. `ORDER BY phone_number ASC NULLS LAST` sorts non-null values in ascending order and places NULL values at the end of the result set. A is incorrect because by default, Spark SQL places NULLs first with ASC ordering. C is a workaround that may produce incorrect alphabetical placement if real phone numbers sort after 'ZZZ', and it changes the actual values returned. D is incorrect because `DESC NULLS FIRST` would sort non-null values in descending order (highest first) and place NULLs first, which is the opposite of the requirement.
questions.reference: ORDER BY with NULLS FIRST / NULLS LAST in Spark SQL

questions.stem: A data engineer has a column `metadata` of type `MAP<STRING, STRING>` containing keys like `'color'`, `'size'`, and `'weight'`. Select TWO valid ways to extract the value for the key `'color'` from each row. (Select TWO)

options.text: `metadata['color']`
`metadata.color`
`element_at(metadata, 'color')`
`EXPLODE(metadata)` followed by `WHERE key = 'color'`

questions.explanation: A and C are correct. For MAP type columns in Spark SQL, two valid extraction methods are: (1) bracket notation `metadata['color']` which returns the value for the specified key, and (2) the `element_at(map, key)` function which does the same thing. Both return NULL if the key is not present. B is incorrect because dot notation (e.g., `metadata.color`) is used for accessing fields within a STRUCT, not keys within a MAP. D is incorrect because while `EXPLODE(metadata)` would work, it creates multiple rows per original row (one per key-value pair), changing the cardinality — it is not a direct value extraction.
questions.reference: MAP type access in Spark SQL: bracket notation and element_at

questions.stem:
A data engineer defines a SQL UDF:

```sql
CREATE FUNCTION dev.utilities.clean_phone(raw_phone STRING)
RETURNS STRING
RETURN regexp_replace(raw_phone, '[^0-9]', '');
```

A colleague in a different team tries to call this function but gets a 'function not found' error. What is the MOST LIKELY cause?

options.text: SQL UDFs are session-scoped and were dropped when the original engineer's session ended
The colleague's session is using a different catalog or schema context, so the function must be called with its fully qualified name `dev.utilities.clean_phone()`
SQL UDFs created with `CREATE FUNCTION` can only be called by the user who created them
`regexp_replace` is not supported inside SQL UDFs in Databricks

questions.explanation: B is correct. SQL UDFs are stored in the metastore under a specific catalog and schema (in this case, `dev.utilities`). If the colleague's session has a different default catalog or schema set (e.g., `prod.analytics`), calling `clean_phone()` without qualification will not find the function. The fix is to call it with the fully qualified three-level name: `dev.utilities.clean_phone(raw_phone)`. A is incorrect because SQL UDFs created with `CREATE FUNCTION` are persisted in the metastore — they are not session-scoped. C is incorrect because any user with EXECUTE privilege can call the function. D is incorrect because `regexp_replace` is a valid built-in function usable inside SQL UDFs.
questions.reference: SQL UDFs: fully qualified names in Unity Catalog

questions.stem:
A data engineer reads a Delta table with:

```python
df = spark.read.table('prod.sales.orders')
```

Vs. a colleague who reads the same table with:

```python
df = spark.read.format('delta').load('s3://bucket/prod/sales/orders')
```

Which statement about these two approaches is correct?

options.text: `spark.read.table()` is slower because it queries the metastore first; the path-based approach reads directly
Both read the same underlying data; `spark.read.table()` uses the metastore to resolve the table location, while `spark.read.format('delta').load()` uses the path directly
`spark.read.format('delta').load()` with a path always reads a snapshot older than what `spark.read.table()` returns
The two approaches return different schemas because the metastore and the path-based reader apply different type casting rules

questions.explanation: B is correct. Both approaches read the same underlying Delta data — the difference is how the table location is resolved. `spark.read.table('prod.sales.orders')` looks up the table in the Unity Catalog metastore to find the storage path, then reads the data. `spark.read.format('delta').load('s3://...')` reads the Delta table directly from the path without a metastore lookup. The resulting DataFrame is identical. A is incorrect because the metastore lookup is negligible and both approaches have similar performance. C is incorrect because both read the current version of the Delta table. D is incorrect because both readers return the same schema as defined in the Delta transaction log.
questions.reference: Reading Delta tables: table name vs path-based access

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to read a nested JSON field where `events` is an array of objects, each containing `event_type` and `timestamp`. They want a final flat table with one row per event. Which sequence of operations achieves this?

options.text: `df.select('user_id', TRANSFORM('events', e -> e.event_type))`
`df.select('user_id', explode('events').alias('event')).select('user_id', 'event.event_type', 'event.timestamp')`
`df.select('user_id', FLATTEN('events'))`
`df.groupBy('user_id').agg(collect_list('events').alias('all_events'))`

questions.explanation: B is correct. `explode('events')` converts each element of the `events` array into a separate row, producing a struct column named `event`. The second `select` then extracts the fields from the struct using dot notation (`event.event_type`, `event.timestamp`), resulting in a flat table with one row per event per user. A is incorrect because `TRANSFORM` is a higher-order function that maps each element to a value but keeps the array structure — it does not create multiple rows. C is incorrect because `FLATTEN` converts a nested array-of-arrays into a flat array but does not explode into rows, and `events` is already a flat array. D is incorrect because `collect_list` further nests the array structure rather than flattening it.
questions.reference: Exploding nested arrays to flat rows in PySpark

questions.stem: A permanent view `vw_active_customers` is created in the `prod.sales` schema. A user drops it with `DROP VIEW prod.sales.vw_active_customers`. What happens to the underlying data?

options.text: The underlying table that the view queries is also dropped
Nothing happens to the underlying data — only the view definition is removed from the metastore
The underlying data is archived to DBFS before the view is dropped
The drop fails because permanent views cannot be dropped without admin privileges

questions.explanation: B is correct. A view is just a stored query definition — it contains no data of its own. Dropping a view removes only the view definition from the metastore. The underlying tables and their data are completely unaffected. A is incorrect because views are independent of their source tables — dropping a view never drops the underlying tables. C is incorrect because no archiving occurs; drop is immediate and only affects the metadata. D is incorrect because the owner of a view (or users with appropriate privileges) can drop it without admin privileges.
questions.reference: Views: DROP VIEW does not affect underlying data

questions.stem: A data engineer configures an Auto Loader stream to ingest CSV files from `s3://bucket/raw/` into a Delta table. New files arrive in the bucket every 10 minutes. What does the `checkpointLocation` store to enable fault tolerance?

options.text: A copy of each processed CSV file for backup purposes
The stream's progress: which files have been processed and the current read position, enabling the stream to resume after a failure
The inferred schema of the CSV files
A list of all S3 bucket paths that are authorized for reading

questions.explanation: B is correct. The `checkpointLocation` for a Structured Streaming job stores metadata about the stream's progress — specifically which source files have been processed (for Auto Loader) and the current offsets/positions. This allows the stream to resume exactly where it stopped after a failure or restart, without reprocessing already-handled data. A is incorrect because the checkpoint stores metadata, not copies of the data files. C is incorrect because schema inference state is controlled by `cloudFiles.schemaLocation`, which is separate from `checkpointLocation`. D is incorrect because S3 authorization is managed through IAM policies, not the checkpoint.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming: checkpointLocation for fault tolerance

questions.stem:
An Auto Loader stream encounters a new JSON file that contains a column `new_field` not previously seen in any processed file. The stream was configured with:

```python
.option('cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode', 'addNewColumns')
```

What happens?

options.text: The stream fails with a schema mismatch error and must be manually restarted with an updated schema
The stream automatically adds `new_field` to the target Delta table schema and continues processing
The new column is silently dropped and the file is processed without `new_field`
The file is quarantined in the `_rescued_data` column and not processed until schema is manually updated

questions.explanation: B is correct. When `cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode` is set to `'addNewColumns'`, Auto Loader automatically detects new columns in incoming files and adds them to both the tracked schema (stored in `cloudFiles.schemaLocation`) and the target Delta table. The stream handles this gracefully without interruption. A is incorrect because schema evolution mode is specifically designed to avoid manual intervention. C is incorrect because with `addNewColumns` mode, the column is preserved, not dropped. D is incorrect because the `_rescued_data` column is used when data cannot be parsed to the existing schema (rescue mode), not for new column evolution.
questions.reference: Auto Loader: schemaEvolutionMode addNewColumns

questions.stem: A data engineer uses `COPY INTO` to load Parquet files and notices that the command loaded 500 files on the first run. On the second run (with no new files), the engineer sees `0 rows affected`. Why?

options.text: The second run failed silently because `COPY INTO` requires a `FORCE` option to run more than once
`COPY INTO` tracks previously loaded files and skips them — since no new files arrived, there is nothing to process
The Delta table was locked for writing by the first `COPY INTO` run and the second run was blocked
`COPY INTO` deletes source files after loading them, so no files remain for the second run

questions.explanation: B is correct. `COPY INTO` maintains a record of which files have been successfully loaded into the target Delta table. On subsequent runs, it queries this file tracking information and skips any files already loaded. If no new files exist in the source path, the result is `0 rows affected` — this is expected and correct behavior. A is incorrect because `COPY INTO` runs normally without a `FORCE` option and the `0 rows affected` is not an error. C is incorrect because Delta Lake supports concurrent operations via its transaction log; no permanent lock is held. D is incorrect because `COPY INTO` never modifies or deletes source files.
questions.reference: COPY INTO: file tracking and idempotent behavior

questions.stem: A streaming pipeline reads from an Auto Loader source and writes to a Delta table using `trigger(availableNow=True)`. How does this trigger behave?

options.text: The stream runs as a continuous long-running job, processing data as it arrives in real time
The stream processes all available data at the time of triggering (including all backlogged files), then stops automatically
The stream processes exactly one micro-batch and stops, regardless of how much data is available
`trigger(availableNow=True)` is deprecated and should be replaced with `trigger(once=True)`

questions.explanation: B is correct. `trigger(availableNow=True)` was introduced as an improvement over `trigger(once=True)`. It processes ALL data available at the time it starts (which may span multiple micro-batches for large backlogs), then terminates the stream when caught up. This is useful for scheduled batch-like ingestion using a streaming API. A is incorrect because this trigger does not run continuously — it terminates after processing all available data. C is incorrect — that is the behavior of `trigger(once=True)`, which processes only one micro-batch then stops. D is incorrect because `availableNow=True` is the newer, recommended replacement for `trigger(once=True)`, not deprecated.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming: trigger(availableNow=True)

questions.stem: A data engineer is deciding between `COPY INTO` and Auto Loader for a new pipeline. Select TWO scenarios where Auto Loader is the BETTER choice compared to `COPY INTO`. (Select TWO)

options.text: Continuously ingesting millions of new files per hour as they arrive in real time
Loading a one-time historical backfill of 10,000 files idempotently
Ingesting files from a deeply nested directory structure where new subdirectories are created daily
Running a SQL-only pipeline where no Python code is available

questions.explanation: A and C are correct. Auto Loader is designed for continuous, scalable file ingestion: (A) it uses cloud notification services (SQS/Event Grid) to efficiently detect and process millions of new files per hour without listing the entire directory, which `COPY INTO` would struggle with at that scale; and (C) Auto Loader's directory listing and notification modes handle new subdirectories automatically as part of the continuous stream. B is incorrect — for a one-time historical backfill, `COPY INTO` is simpler and more appropriate since its idempotent SQL-based approach is straightforward for a finite file set. D is incorrect — Auto Loader requires Python/PySpark code and is not available as a pure SQL command; `COPY INTO` is the SQL-native option.
questions.reference: Auto Loader vs COPY INTO: choosing the right tool

questions.stem: A Structured Streaming query writes to a Delta table in `outputMode('complete')`. What is a key limitation of this mode for large streaming aggregations?

options.text: Complete mode only works with Kafka sources and is not supported for Delta table sources
Complete mode rewrites the entire aggregation result to the sink on every micro-batch, which grows increasingly expensive as the aggregation state accumulates more groups
Complete mode cannot be used with Delta table sinks — it requires a JDBC sink
Complete mode disables fault tolerance and does not use the checkpoint location

questions.explanation: B is correct. In complete output mode, Spark writes the full aggregation result (all groups, all time windows) to the output sink with every micro-batch. As the stream runs longer and accumulates more unique groups (e.g., more users, more products), the amount of data written per micro-batch grows proportionally. This makes complete mode impractical for high-cardinality aggregations over long-running streams. A is incorrect because complete mode works with any streaming source. C is incorrect because Delta is a fully supported sink for complete mode. D is incorrect because complete mode still uses checkpointing for fault tolerance.
questions.reference: Streaming output modes: complete mode scalability limitation

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to configure a Databricks Job to send an email if a task succeeds. Where is this configured?

options.text: In the cluster's advanced configuration under 'Spark Environment Variables'
In the Job's 'Notifications' settings, by adding an email address for the 'on success' event
By adding a final notebook task to the job that sends an email using Python
In the workspace admin console under 'Email Notifications'

questions.explanation: B is correct. Databricks Jobs has a built-in 'Notifications' section in the job configuration where you can add email addresses to be notified on specific events: task start, task success, task failure, job failure, and job duration exceeded. This is the native, no-code way to configure email notifications. A is incorrect because Spark environment variables control cluster behavior, not notification routing. C is incorrect because while a custom email task is possible, it is an anti-pattern when native notifications exist and requires the success of the job to reach that task. D is incorrect because there is no 'Email Notifications' section in the workspace admin console for job events.
questions.reference: Databricks Jobs: success notifications

options.text: The pipeline fails immediately and stops processing
The record is dropped from `silver_transactions` and not written to the output table
The record is written to `silver_transactions` and the violation is recorded in pipeline metrics as a warning
The record is automatically corrected to `amount = 0` before being written

questions.explanation: C is correct. `@dlt.expect` is the 'warn only' expectation in Delta Live Tables. When a record violates the expectation, it is still written to the output table, but the violation is counted and reported in the DLT pipeline metrics dashboard. This allows engineers to monitor data quality without interrupting the pipeline. A is incorrect — that is `@dlt.expect_or_fail` behavior. B is incorrect — that is `@dlt.expect_or_drop` behavior. D is incorrect because DLT expectations are declarative quality checks, not data transformation rules — they never automatically modify values.
questions.reference: DLT expectations: @dlt.expect (warn mode)

questions.stem: A data engineer is designing a Databricks multi-task job for a daily ETL pipeline. Select TWO valid task types that can be used within a Databricks Job. (Select TWO)

options.text: Notebook task — runs a Databricks notebook
Delta Live Tables pipeline task — triggers an existing DLT pipeline
SQL Dashboard task — renders a Databricks SQL dashboard as a scheduled refresh
REST API task — calls an external REST endpoint as part of the job

questions.explanation: A and B are correct. Databricks Jobs supports several task types: Notebook (runs a notebook), Python script (runs a .py file on the cluster), JAR (runs a Java/Scala JAR), Delta Live Tables pipeline (triggers a DLT pipeline run), SQL (runs a SQL file or query), dbt (runs a dbt project), and Spark Submit. Both notebook tasks (A) and DLT pipeline tasks (B) are explicitly supported. C is incorrect because Databricks Jobs does not have a native 'SQL Dashboard' task type — dashboards have their own refresh scheduling. D is incorrect because there is no native 'REST API' task type in Databricks Jobs (though a notebook task can call a REST API via Python code).
questions.reference: Databricks Jobs: supported task types

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to configure a Databricks Job to run every Monday at 9:00 AM UTC. Which cron expression correctly represents this schedule?
questions.explanation: A is correct. Standard cron syntax is `minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week`. The expression `0 9 * * 1` means: minute=0, hour=9, any day of month, any month, day-of-week=1 (Monday). This runs at 9:00 AM every Monday. B is incorrect because the minute and hour fields are swapped (`9 0` would be 0:09 AM, not 9:00 AM). C is incorrect because the third field is day-of-month, not hour — `0 0 9 * MON` would mean midnight on the 9th of every month that is also a Monday. D is incorrect because `* 9 * * 1` means every minute during the 9 AM hour on Mondays, not just at 9:00 AM.
questions.reference: Databricks Jobs: cron expression syntax

questions.stem: In Unity Catalog, what is a 'metastore'?

options.text: A physical storage bucket that holds all Delta table data files
The top-level metadata repository for a Unity Catalog deployment that manages all catalogs, schemas, tables, and access control
A specific schema within a catalog that stores system metadata tables
A type of Databricks cluster optimized for metadata-heavy workloads

questions.explanation: B is correct. The Unity Catalog metastore is the top-level governance container for a Databricks account region. It stores all metadata (catalog, schema, and table definitions) and manages permissions and access control for all objects registered within it. Typically, one metastore is assigned per Databricks workspace region. A is incorrect because the metastore stores metadata, not data files — the actual files live in cloud object storage. C is incorrect because the metastore is above the catalog level in the hierarchy, not a schema within a catalog. D is incorrect because a metastore is a logical concept, not a cluster type.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog: metastore overview

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to add custom metadata to a Delta table to record the source system it was ingested from. Which SQL command correctly adds a table property?

options.text: `ALTER TABLE prod.sales.orders SET TBLPROPERTIES ('source_system' = 'salesforce')`
`UPDATE TABLE prod.sales.orders SET METADATA source_system = 'salesforce'`
`COMMENT ON TABLE prod.sales.orders IS 'source_system: salesforce'`
`INSERT INTO prod.sales.orders TBLPROPERTIES ('source_system', 'salesforce')`

questions.explanation: A is correct. `ALTER TABLE ... SET TBLPROPERTIES (...)` is the standard SQL command for adding or updating key-value metadata properties on a Delta table. These properties are stored in the Delta transaction log and can be read with `DESCRIBE DETAIL` or `SHOW TBLPROPERTIES`. B is incorrect because `UPDATE TABLE ... SET METADATA` is not valid SQL syntax. C is incorrect because `COMMENT ON TABLE` sets the table description/comment, which is a single string — it is not the right mechanism for structured key-value properties. D is incorrect because `INSERT INTO ... TBLPROPERTIES` is not valid SQL syntax.
questions.reference: Table properties: TBLPROPERTIES via ALTER TABLE

questions.stem:
A data engineer creates an external table:

```sql
CREATE TABLE prod.raw.clickstream
USING DELTA
LOCATION 's3://data-lake/clickstream/';
```

Another engineer then runs `DROP TABLE prod.raw.clickstream`. What is the state of the data in `s3://data-lake/clickstream/` after the drop?

options.text: The data files are deleted because `DROP TABLE` always removes all associated data
The data files remain intact in S3; only the table's metadata entry is removed from the Unity Catalog metastore
The data files are moved to a Unity Catalog-managed location as a backup
The drop fails because external tables cannot be dropped without the `PURGE` keyword

questions.explanation: B is correct. External tables in Unity Catalog (created with a `LOCATION` clause pointing to user-managed storage) are dropped without deleting the underlying data files. Only the metadata registration in the Unity Catalog metastore is removed. The data files at `s3://data-lake/clickstream/` continue to exist after the drop. This is intentional: the data predates or exists independently of the table registration. A is incorrect because data deletion on drop applies to managed tables, not external tables. C is incorrect because Unity Catalog does not move external table data during a drop. D is incorrect because external tables can be dropped with a standard `DROP TABLE` without any `PURGE` keyword.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog external tables: DROP TABLE preserves data

questions.stem: A data engineer runs `SHOW TBLPROPERTIES prod.sales.orders`. Select TWO types of information this command can return. (Select TWO)

options.text: Custom metadata properties added with `ALTER TABLE ... SET TBLPROPERTIES`
Delta configuration properties such as `delta.minReaderVersion` and `delta.enableChangeDataFeed`
The full history of all DML operations performed on the table
The list of all users who have been granted SELECT on the table

questions.explanation: A and B are correct. `SHOW TBLPROPERTIES` returns key-value pairs stored as table properties, which includes both: (A) user-defined custom properties added via `ALTER TABLE ... SET TBLPROPERTIES`, and (B) Delta Lake protocol properties automatically set on Delta tables (such as `delta.minReaderVersion`, `delta.minWriterVersion`, `delta.enableChangeDataFeed`, etc.). C is incorrect because table operation history is returned by `DESCRIBE HISTORY`, not `SHOW TBLPROPERTIES`. D is incorrect because privilege listings require `SHOW GRANTS ON TABLE ...` commands.
questions.reference: SHOW TBLPROPERTIES: custom and Delta properties

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to programmatically run another notebook from within a notebook and retrieve its return value. They want the called notebook to complete before execution continues in the calling notebook. Which utility should they use?

options.text: `%run ./other_notebook` — this executes the notebook and returns its last cell output to the calling notebook
`dbutils.notebook.run('./other_notebook', timeout_seconds=300)` — this executes the notebook synchronously and returns its exit value
`spark.submit('./other_notebook')` — this submits the notebook as a Spark job and waits for completion
`dbutils.fs.run('./other_notebook')` — this runs the notebook in the filesystem context

questions.explanation: B is correct. `dbutils.notebook.run(path, timeout_seconds, arguments)` programmatically runs another notebook and waits for it to complete (synchronous execution). The called notebook can return a string value via `dbutils.notebook.exit(value)`, which is returned to the calling notebook. The `timeout_seconds` parameter specifies the maximum wait time. A is incorrect because `%run` executes the notebook inline and imports its variable scope, but it does not support retrieving a structured return value via `dbutils.notebook.exit`. C is incorrect because `spark.submit` is not a valid method for notebooks. D is incorrect because `dbutils.fs` is for filesystem operations, not notebook execution.
questions.reference: dbutils.notebook.run() for programmatic notebook execution

questions.stem: A Databricks workspace is configured with Unity Catalog. An admin needs to create a new storage location that allows tables to be stored in an S3 bucket outside of the default metastore storage. Which Unity Catalog object enables this?

options.text: A Unity Catalog Mount Point, which is registered with `dbutils.fs.mount()`
An External Location, which associates a cloud storage path with a Storage Credential to allow Unity Catalog to access it
A managed location override configured in the workspace-level cluster policy
A DBFS root path alias that maps a custom prefix to an S3 bucket

questions.explanation: B is correct. In Unity Catalog, an External Location defines a specific cloud storage path (e.g., `s3://my-bucket/path/`) and links it to a Storage Credential (which holds the IAM role or service principal with S3 access). Once an External Location is registered, it can be used as the `LOCATION` for external tables or as a managed storage location for a catalog or schema. A is incorrect because DBFS mounts (`dbutils.fs.mount`) are a workspace-level feature that pre-dates Unity Catalog and does not provide the same governance capabilities. C is incorrect because cluster policies control cluster configuration, not storage paths. D is incorrect because there is no 'DBFS root path alias' concept in Unity Catalog.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog: External Locations and Storage Credentials

questions.stem: A data engineering team is evaluating whether to use a job cluster or an existing all-purpose cluster for their nightly batch ETL job. Which statement BEST describes the trade-off between these two options?

options.text: Job clusters are always more expensive than all-purpose clusters because they require dedicated hardware

Job clusters are cost-optimized for automated workloads (terminated after the job, fresh environment each run) but have startup overhead; all-purpose clusters avoid startup time but accrue cost when idle between jobs
All-purpose clusters support autoscaling but job clusters do not, making all-purpose clusters always more scalable
Job clusters share the cluster state between runs, which makes them faster for iterative jobs

questions.explanation: B is correct. The key trade-offs are: job clusters start fresh for each run (no stale state, clean library environment), are terminated after the job completes (no idle cost), and have a startup time overhead. All-purpose clusters are already running (no startup time), but they continue to accrue compute cost during idle periods between jobs. For nightly batch jobs, the cost savings of job clusters typically outweigh the startup overhead. A is incorrect because job clusters are generally cheaper due to no idle cost — they are billed only for active run time. C is incorrect because job clusters do support autoscaling. D is incorrect because job clusters do NOT share state between runs; each run starts with a fresh cluster — this is actually a benefit (clean state), not the reason they are 'faster'.
questions.reference: Job cluster vs all-purpose cluster: cost and lifecycle trade-offs

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to install a Python library on a Databricks cluster so it is available for all notebooks that attach to the cluster. Where should they configure this?

options.text: Run `pip install <library>` in a notebook cell — the library is automatically available cluster-wide and persists after cluster restart
Add the library in the cluster's 'Libraries' tab (or in the cluster configuration YAML), which installs it on all nodes at cluster start
Add the library to the DBFS root and Databricks will auto-detect and install it when notebooks are attached
Use `%sh conda install <library>` in a notebook cell to install it on the driver node cluster-wide

questions.explanation: B is correct. Cluster-level libraries are configured in the cluster's 'Libraries' tab in the Databricks UI or in the cluster specification YAML. When the cluster starts, Databricks installs the specified libraries on all nodes (driver and workers) so they are available to every notebook attached to that cluster. A is incorrect because `pip install` in a notebook cell installs the library only for the current notebook's Python session and does not propagate to worker nodes for distributed Spark UDFs; it also does not persist after cluster restart. C is incorrect because Databricks does not auto-detect libraries placed in DBFS for cluster-level installation. D is incorrect because `%sh conda install` runs on the driver node only and does not persist across restarts or install on worker nodes.
questions.reference: Databricks cluster libraries: cluster-level library installation

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to find the top 3 customers by total spend within each region, without filtering out other rows. Which SQL feature is MOST appropriate?

options.text: `GROUP BY region ORDER BY total_spend DESC LIMIT 3`
`RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY total_spend DESC)` and filter where rank <= 3
`FILTER(customers, c -> c.rank <= 3)` applied after a sort
`EXPLODE(customers)` followed by ordering and limiting to 3

questions.explanation: B is correct. Window functions like `RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY total_spend DESC)` compute a rank for each customer within their region. You can then wrap this in a subquery or CTE and filter `WHERE rank <= 3` to get the top 3 per region. This correctly handles ties and does not collapse the result to one row per region. A is incorrect because `GROUP BY region LIMIT 3` returns at most 3 regions' aggregated totals, not the top 3 customers within each region. C is incorrect because `FILTER` is a higher-order function for array columns, not for row-level ranking across a table. D is incorrect because `EXPLODE` is for array/map columns and has no role in this ranking problem.
questions.reference: Window functions: RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY) for top-N per group

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to combine the results of two SELECT statements, returning all rows from both without removing duplicates. Which set operator should they use?

options.text: `UNION` — combines results and removes duplicate rows
`UNION ALL` — combines results and keeps all rows including duplicates
`INTERSECT` — returns only rows present in both result sets
`EXCEPT` — returns rows from the first query not present in the second

questions.explanation: B is correct. `UNION ALL` concatenates the results of two SELECT statements and retains all rows, including duplicates. This is the most efficient set union operator because it does not require a deduplication pass. A is incorrect because plain `UNION` (without `ALL`) performs an implicit `DISTINCT` operation, removing duplicate rows — this is slower and changes the result when duplicates should be preserved. C is incorrect because `INTERSECT` returns only rows common to both result sets. D is incorrect because `EXCEPT` (or `MINUS`) returns rows from the first query that are not present in the second — a set difference, not a union.
questions.reference: Set operators in Spark SQL: UNION vs UNION ALL

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to find all orders where the `product_id` appears in a curated list stored in a `featured_products` table. Which query correctly uses a subquery with `IN` to accomplish this?

options.text: `SELECT * FROM orders WHERE product_id IN (SELECT product_id FROM featured_products)`
`SELECT * FROM orders INNER JOIN featured_products USING (product_id) WHERE IN featured_products`
`SELECT * FROM orders WHERE product_id = ANY featured_products`
`SELECT * FROM orders, featured_products WHERE orders.product_id CONTAINS featured_products.product_id`

questions.explanation: A is correct. The `IN (subquery)` syntax in Spark SQL allows filtering rows where a column's value appears in the result set returned by a subquery. `WHERE product_id IN (SELECT product_id FROM featured_products)` returns all orders whose `product_id` is in the `featured_products` table. B is incorrect because the `WHERE IN featured_products` clause after the JOIN is not valid SQL syntax. C is incorrect because `= ANY featured_products` is not valid Spark SQL syntax for this use case — `ANY` with a subquery has different syntax and semantics. D is incorrect because `CONTAINS` is not a valid SQL operator for comparing scalar column values against a table.
questions.reference: Subquery with IN operator in Spark SQL

questions.stem:
A data engineer creates a bucketed table:

```python
(df.write
  .format('parquet')
  .bucketBy(8, 'customer_id')
  .sortBy('customer_id')
  .saveAsTable('bucketed_orders'))
```

Select TWO statements that correctly describe the effect of bucketing. (Select TWO)

options.text: Data is physically co-located by `customer_id` hash into 8 bucket files, which can eliminate the shuffle in joins on `customer_id` when both sides are bucketed identically
Bucketing creates a directory structure like `customer_id=value/` similar to partitioning, enabling partition pruning
When joining two tables bucketed by the same column with the same number of buckets, Spark can perform a bucket merge join without a full shuffle
Bucketing compresses data using a specialized algorithm to reduce storage size

questions.explanation: A and C are correct. Bucketing (or hash bucketing) distributes data into a fixed number of files based on a hash of the specified column(s). When two tables are bucketed by the same column(s) with the same number of buckets, Spark can join corresponding bucket files directly without performing a shuffle — because the data is already co-partitioned (option A and C describe the same benefit from different angles). B is incorrect because bucketing does NOT create partition directories named `column=value/` — that is what `partitionBy()` does. Bucketing creates flat files with hash-based naming, not directory hierarchies. D is incorrect because bucketing controls how data is distributed across files, not how it is compressed.
questions.reference: DataFrame write: bucketBy() for shuffle-free joins

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to join two streaming DataFrames — `click_events` and `purchase_events` — on `session_id`. Both are streaming sources. Which statement about stream-stream joins is correct?

options.text: Stream-stream joins are not supported in Structured Streaming; at least one side must be a static batch source
Stream-stream joins are supported but require a watermark on both sides to bound the state that must be maintained for matching late-arriving events
Stream-stream joins always produce exactly one output row per event from either stream, even if no match is found
Stream-stream joins automatically deduplicate matched rows by session_id without any additional configuration

questions.explanation: B is correct. Structured Streaming does support stream-stream joins, but they require careful state management. Because both sides are continuously arriving, Spark must buffer events from both streams to find matches. Without watermarks, the state buffer grows indefinitely. Adding watermarks to both streams allows Spark to bound the state by specifying how late events can arrive — once past the watermark, unmatched events are dropped and their state is cleaned up. A is incorrect because stream-stream joins are a supported Structured Streaming feature. C is incorrect because stream-stream joins only emit rows where a match is found (for inner joins) — unmatched events do not produce output. D is incorrect because stream-stream joins do not automatically deduplicate; deduplication requires additional logic using `dropDuplicates`.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming: stream-stream joins and watermark requirements

questions.stem: A Delta table has the Change Data Feed (CDF) feature enabled. A data engineer wants to read only the changes (inserts, updates, deletes) that occurred since version 10. Which code correctly reads these changes as a streaming source?

options.text: `spark.readStream.format('delta').option('startingVersion', 10).load('/path/to/table')`
`spark.readStream.format('delta').option('readChangeFeed', 'true').option('startingVersion', 10).load('/path/to/table')`
`spark.readStream.format('deltaChangeFeed').option('startingVersion', 10).load('/path/to/table')`
`spark.read.format('delta').option('versionAsOf', 10).load('/path/to/table')`

questions.explanation: B is correct. To read a Delta table's Change Data Feed as a stream, you must set `.option('readChangeFeed', 'true')` along with a starting position (either `startingVersion` or `startingTimestamp`). The CDF stream produces rows with additional metadata columns (`_change_type`, `_commit_version`, `_commit_timestamp`) indicating whether each row was inserted, updated (pre-image or post-image), or deleted. A is incorrect because without `readChangeFeed`, this reads the table's insert-only stream (new files added), not the full change feed including updates and deletes. C is incorrect because `'deltaChangeFeed'` is not a valid format name. D is incorrect because `spark.read` (without `Stream`) creates a batch read using time travel, not a streaming change feed.
questions.reference: Delta Change Data Feed: streaming CDF reads

questions.stem: A data engineer configures a streaming job with `trigger(once=True)`. What is the behavior of this trigger?

options.text: The stream runs continuously but limits each micro-batch to exactly one record
The stream processes one micro-batch of all available data and then terminates automatically
The stream runs for exactly one minute before stopping
`trigger(once=True)` and `trigger(availableNow=True)` are identical in behavior

questions.explanation: B is correct. `trigger(once=True)` starts the stream, processes a single micro-batch containing all currently available data, and then terminates. This enables a batch-style execution pattern using the streaming API with all the benefits of checkpointing (exactly-once delivery guarantees). A is incorrect because the trigger has nothing to do with the number of records per batch — it limits to one micro-batch execution. C is incorrect because the trigger is not time-based in the 'run for one minute' sense. D is incorrect because while both `trigger(once=True)` and `trigger(availableNow=True)` stop after processing available data, they differ: `availableNow=True` may run multiple micro-batches to process a large backlog, while `once=True` processes exactly one micro-batch then stops.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming: trigger(once=True) behavior

questions.stem: A data engineer is deciding between a job cluster and an existing all-purpose cluster for a Delta Live Tables pipeline. Which statement is MOST accurate about DLT pipeline cluster management?

options.text: DLT pipelines must always use an existing all-purpose cluster that the engineer creates and manages manually
DLT pipelines create and manage their own cluster automatically; the engineer configures cluster properties in the pipeline settings rather than creating a cluster manually
DLT pipelines only support serverless compute and cannot use classic clusters with custom instance types
DLT pipeline clusters must be created 24 hours in advance using the Clusters UI before a pipeline can run

questions.explanation: B is correct. Delta Live Tables manages its own cluster lifecycle. When a pipeline runs, DLT automatically creates a cluster based on the pipeline's configuration (instance type, number of workers, Photon, etc.) and terminates it after the run in production mode (or keeps it running in development mode). Engineers do not manually create or attach clusters to DLT pipelines — they configure cluster properties within the pipeline settings JSON or UI. A is incorrect because DLT pipelines do NOT attach to manually-created all-purpose clusters; they provision their own clusters. C is incorrect because while serverless compute is an option, DLT also supports classic clusters with custom configurations. D is incorrect because DLT creates clusters on demand at pipeline execution time, not 24 hours in advance.
questions.reference: DLT pipeline cluster management: automatic provisioning

options.text: In the `default` schema of the current catalog, since `target` only affects streaming tables
In the `prod_db` schema (database) of the current catalog, as specified by the pipeline's `target` configuration
In a temporary schema that is dropped when the pipeline run completes
In the DLT pipeline's internal storage location, inaccessible outside the pipeline

questions.explanation: B is correct. The `target` field in a DLT pipeline's configuration specifies the schema (database) where all tables defined in the pipeline are published (materialized). In this case, `silver_orders` will be created in the `prod_db` schema of the current catalog (as set by the pipeline's `catalog` configuration, or the workspace default catalog). All DLT tables from this pipeline will appear in `prod_db` and can be queried by other users with appropriate permissions. A is incorrect because `target` applies to all DLT tables, not only streaming ones. C is incorrect because the `target` schema is a persistent, user-managed schema — tables persist after the pipeline run. D is incorrect because DLT publishes tables to a user-accessible schema, not an internal inaccessible location.
questions.reference: DLT pipeline configuration: target schema for table publication

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to query Unity Catalog system metadata to list all tables in the `prod` catalog that they have access to. Which approach is MOST appropriate?

options.text: `SHOW TABLES IN prod.*` — lists all tables in all schemas in the prod catalog
`SELECT * FROM prod.information_schema.tables` — queries the Unity Catalog information schema for table metadata
`DESCRIBE CATALOG prod` — returns all tables and schemas in the prod catalog
`SHOW HISTORY prod` — shows all objects created in the prod catalog with their creation timestamps

questions.explanation: B is correct. Unity Catalog exposes an `information_schema` within each catalog that contains standard SQL information schema views, including `tables`, `columns`, `views`, `schemata`, and more. Querying `prod.information_schema.tables` returns metadata about all tables in the `prod` catalog that the current user has access to — including table name, schema, table type, and comment. A is incorrect because `SHOW TABLES IN prod.*` is not valid syntax; you can only `SHOW TABLES IN <schema>`, not a wildcard across all schemas. C is incorrect because `DESCRIBE CATALOG` returns catalog-level metadata (comments, properties), not a list of all tables. D is incorrect because `SHOW HISTORY` is not a valid Unity Catalog command; it is `DESCRIBE HISTORY` and it applies to individual Delta tables.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog: information_schema for metadata discovery

questions.stem: A security engineer asks a Databricks data engineer whether Unity Catalog supports a `DENY` privilege — a command that explicitly blocks a user from accessing an object even if they have been granted access through a group. What is the correct answer?

options.text: `DENY` is supported in Unity Catalog and takes precedence over any `GRANT` on the same object
`DENY` does not exist in Unity Catalog. Access is controlled purely by the presence or absence of `GRANT` — if a user has no grant (directly or through a group), they have no access
`DENY` is supported but only at the catalog level, not at the table or schema level
`DENY` must be used to revoke access; `REVOKE` is not a supported command in Unity Catalog

questions.explanation: B is correct. Unity Catalog uses a pure grant-based access control model — there is no `DENY` command. Access to an object is determined by whether the user (directly or through group membership) has been granted the necessary privileges. If a user needs to be blocked from accessing a resource they currently have access to via a group grant, the correct approach is to remove them from the group or to restructure grants — not to apply a DENY. This is a common misconception from engineers familiar with systems like SQL Server or Azure Synapse that do support an explicit DENY. A is incorrect because DENY does not exist in Unity Catalog. C is incorrect for the same reason. D is incorrect because `REVOKE` is the correct and supported command for removing grants in Unity Catalog — it is the counterpart to `GRANT`.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog: no DENY privilege — grant-based access control only
